coredata.dc:title: Boerhaavia diffusa derived carbon quantum dots: A fluorescence turnoff sensor for ferric ion detection in aqueous solution
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Jose, Jincy
Mohanraj, Rangaswamy
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a one-pot hydrothermal synthesis of Boerhaavia diffusa, generated bright luminescent carbon quantum dot (CQD) was used to provide an effective sensing platform for metal ions. The structure, shape and luminescent properties of the resultant CQDs are characterised by various spectroscopic and electro microscopic techniques. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) demonstrated that the surfaces of CQDs were rich in carbon, nitrogen and oxygen content. The particle size of the produced CQD is about 5 nm according to transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The high quantum yield of the produced CQD was 21.2 % at an excitation wavelength of 380 nm. The synthesised CQDs exhibited remarkable sensing potential in identifying Fe3+ ions in aqueous solution with a detection limit of 0.741 µM. Hence, this study creates fresh avenues for producing high-quality plant based luminous CQDs sensors that satisfy the needs of numerous applications in the field of environmental water analysis that creates a paradigm shift towards a sustainable future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Boerhaavia diffusa
Fluorescence
Carbon dots
Fe3+ ions
XPS

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of B. diffusa derived CQDs
Physical and chemical characteristics of the CQDs derived from B. diffusa
Effect of temperature on the photoluminescence of CQDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India
Indian Council of Forestry Research and Education

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Dr. Rangaswamy Mohanraj is a Professor of Environmental Sciences at Bharathidasan University, Tiruchirappalli, India. His primary research interest lies on Environmental Impact and Risk Assessment, Urban Air Pollution, Hydro-Geochemistry, Ecological Restoration, Forest Carbon Dynamics, and Corporate Environmental Management. As a principal investigator he had completed five major externally funded research projects [projects funded by DST, UGC and DRDO (Govt. of India)]; and presently carrying out two major externally funded projects including DST-SERB (CRG) and an All-India coordinated project funded by ICFRE (MoEF&CC, GoI). He had also completed 25 Environmental Impact Assessment/Consultancy projects as a Principal Investigator and Co-Investigator. He had successfully guided 7 PhD scholars in Environmental Science and Environmental Management. He can be contacted at mohan@bdu.ac.in.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Absorption spectra of B. diffusa derived CQDs. (b) PL emission spectra as a function of wavelength.
FT-IR spectrum of B. diffusa derived CQDs.
XRD pattern of B. diffusa derived CQDs.
Effect of temperature on photoluminescence of CQDs.
(a) HR-TEM image of B. diffusa derived CQDs. (b) SAED pattern of CQDs.
(a) XPS spectra of B. diffusa derived CQDs. (b) C1s fitted peak. (c) N1 s fitted peak. (d) O1 s fitted peak.

Sensing features of B. diffusa derived CQDs (a) Selectivity of CQDs for various metal ions (b) Interference study of Fe3+ ions with various metal ions (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of B. diffusa derived CQDs upon addition of various concentrations of Fe3+ ions (d) Stern–Volmer plot of Fe3+ .
Analytical performance comparison of different CQDs for Fe3+ ions sensing.

head.ce:title: Boerhaavia diffusa derived carbon quantum dots: A fluorescence turnoff sensor for ferric ion detection in aqueous solution

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Environmental Science and Management, Bharathidasan University, Tiruchirappalli 620024, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Environmental Science and Management

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Environmental Science and Management, Bharathidasan University, Tiruchirappalli 620024, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a one-pot hydrothermal synthesis of Boerhaavia diffusa, generated bright luminescent carbon quantum dot (CQD) was used to provide an effective sensing platform for metal ions. The structure, shape and luminescent properties of the resultant CQDs are characterised by various spectroscopic and electro microscopic techniques. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) demonstrated that the surfaces of CQDs were rich in carbon, nitrogen and oxygen content. The particle size of the produced CQD is about 5 nm according to transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The high quantum yield of the produced CQD was 21.2 % at an excitation wavelength of 380 nm. The synthesised CQDs exhibited remarkable sensing potential in identifying Fe3+ ions in aqueous solution with a detection limit of 0.741 µM. Hence, this study creates fresh avenues for producing high-quality plant based luminous CQDs sensors that satisfy the needs of numerous applications in the field of environmental water analysis that creates a paradigm shift towards a sustainable future.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No datasets were generated or analysed during the current study.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon, one of the elements that make up life itself and a sort of element that is plentiful and non-toxic, is used to make carbon quantum dots (or CQDs). CQDs are a novel class of quasi-spherical, crystalline, “zero-dimensional” carbon nanomaterials that have garnered significant attention recently having a spatial size range from 1 nm to 10 nm [1]. CQDs have gained importance in nano chemistry because of their unusual physical attributes, since its first discovery in 2004 during the purification of single-walled carbon nanotube (SWCNT) [2,3]. CQDs feature intense, controllable fluorescence properties similar to inorganic luminous semiconductor nanoparticles. Fluorescent CQDs have a wide range of applications in fields such as biosensors [4], solar cells [5], drug delivery [6], chemosensors [7], electrochemical sensors [8], cell imaging [9], and catalysis [10]. These applications are made possible due to their significant chemical and physical characteristics, which include bright fluorescence, high water solubility, biocompatibility, and zero toxicity [11–15]. The composition and biocompatibility of CQDs make them a promising substitute for metal-based quantum dots. Due to their higher transferability, they are also catalytically active [3] and are being investigated as potential substitutes for conventional fluorescent inorganic quantum dots, which have a number of disadvantages, including heavy metal toxicity, expensiveness, and possible risks to human health and the environment [16].

Several methods have been used in the creation of CQDs so far. Yet, green CQDs manufactured from naturally occurring green materials are becoming more competitive and have drawn interest from researchers all around the world. The natural green resources are inexpensive, readily available, highly stable, have a straightforward protocol, safer, more environmentally friendly, and have an abundance of carbon sources, they are a great choice for the synthesis of CQDs. Scientists have employed a variety of techniques, such as hydrothermal treatment, laser ablation, electrochemical oxidation, chemical oxidation, microwave treatment and sun irradiation to create fluorescent CQDs [17,18]. The hydrothermal treatment in aqueous medium is regarded as most used green synthetic strategy for the synthesis of CQDs [19–21]. Various plant resources and bio-wastes are being used to develop biomass-derived CQDs. These materials include Mangifera indica leaves [18], lemon Juice [22], mint leaf [23], waste tea [24], banana peels [25], garlic [26], sweet potato [27], aloe vera [28], pith of tapioca stem [29], pineapple [30], Rose flowers [31] and Coccinia Grandis [32] were utilized to synthesize the carbon quantum dot material as renewable natural precursors. CQDs have been effectively applied to the detection of metal ions due to their excellent photostability, low toxicity, simple surface modification, and strong biocompatibility. CQDs are used to identify dangerous materials such as metal ions, pesticides, and other chemicals from aqueous solution. The most recent work includes carbon quantum dots derived from leftover kiwi fruit peel as a highly selective fluorescent sensor for ferric ion detection using hydrothermal synthesis with a detection limit of 0.95 µM [33]. Kentucky blue grass was used to produce a novel carbon quantum dot via hydrothermal method for the identification of Fe3+ and Mn2+ ions from aqueous solution with a detection limit of 1.4 and 1.2 μM, respectively [34]. Green synthesis of Chinese herbal residues was used to produce boron derived carbon quantum dots that have been used in the application of Fe3+ sensing, photodegradation and anti-counterfeiting [35]. The endosperm of Borassus flabellifer was utilised to develop carbon quantum dots decorated probe for the sensitive detection of Fe3+ ion sensing in the potable water which indicates the applicability of sensor development. The developed sensor is simple, inexpensive, label-free, quick, and having strong sensitivity and selectivity towards Fe3+ ions from aqueous solution [36]. A novel green synthesized carbon quantum dots were developed using grape skin via hydrothermal method which exhibits uniform particle size, high quantum yield (26.7 %) and very good fluorescence performance for the sensing of Fe3+ and Pb2+ ions from aqueous solution [37]. The carbon quantum dots are produced directly from the most plentiful agricultural biomass such as rice straw using a one-pot method. The resulting carbon quantum dots have good efficiency and high selectivity for Fe3+ ion sensing [38].

Iron is an essential microelement and the most prevalent metal element in the human body. Iron is the third most common metal in enzymes and it is widely accepted that iron is a necessary cofactor for enzyme function [39,40]. It is essential to biological systems for interacting with regulatory proteins in the transport of oxygen and it creates a binding point to take in oxygen from the air and make haemoglobin [41]. Nevertheless, excessive iron ion levels can cause major health issues include cytotoxicity, Parkinson's disease, blood transfusion requirements, and metabolic diseases. Furthermore, because of free or excess iron ions have a propensity to absorb or acquire electrons from their environment, they can also cause harm to cellular processes or form reactive oxygen species [39,42–44]. Hence, it is crucial to develop efficient sensing devices for the qualitative and quantitative assessment of Fe3+ ions for medicinal, clinical and environmental concerns.

In this study, we describe a simple, environmentally friendly, and inexpensive hydrothermal approach for producing CQDs using the Boerhaavia diffusa plant as the precursor. Boerhaavia diffusa, a flowering plant in four o’ clock family commonly known by the name ‘Punarnava’ in Indian medicine which grows all across India's wastelands, ditches, agricultural lands and marshy places. The roots are used to treat anasarca, ascites, and jaundice since they are said to have diuretic and laxative properties [45]. In various communities, the Boerhaavia species have been used medicinally since the B.C. Through phytochemistry, a number of plant products have been identified, and the extract of their various plant sections has been shown to be effective in treating a variety of ailments without having any negative side effects [46]. The plant Boerhaavia diffusa is rich in several compounds, including but not limited to flavonoids, alkaloids, steroids, triterpenoids, lipids, lignins, carbohydrates, proteins, and glycoproteins. [47]. The plant contains 15 amino acids, including 6 necessary amino acids, and 14 amino acids in the root, including 7 essential amino acids. The roots contain about 2 % of the plant's alkaloid content, while the herb and roots are high in lipids and proteins. These chemical constituents could probably be responsible for the functional groups in the carbon quantum dots.

A comprehensive study was conducted against the suitability of the developed CQD with respect to the fluorescence characteristics for Fe3+ detection for metal ion sensing in aqueous solution. Additionally, a thorough examination of the optical and physicochemical characteristics of CQDs has been provided. The present work covers that CQDs play as selective ion detection probes and as far as the authors are aware, this is the first investigation into the synthesis of CQDs employing Boerhaavia diffusa plant, which possess a strong fluorescence and an effective sensitivity for sensing Fe3+ ions. The present work demonstrates the potential of using naturally occurring plant sources to synthesize CQDs for analytical purposes, opening the door for further developments in environmental monitoring applications.

The leaves of Boerhaavia diffusa plant were collected from the local area in Kerala, India in 2023. The AR grade metal salts such as Ca (NO3)2·4H2O (99 %), Hg2(NO3)2·2H2O (≥97 %), KNO3 (≥99 %), Mg (NO3)2·6H2O (≥99 %), Pb (NO3)2 (≥99 %), Sr (NO3)2 (≥99 %), Zn (NO3)2·6H2O (98 %), Cd (NO3)2·4H2O (98 %), Cu (NO3)2·3H2O (98 %), (NH4)2Fe (SO4)2·6H2O (≥98 %), Ni (NO3)2·6H2O (≥97 %), were purchased from Fisher Scientific. Milli-Q water was used in all experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of B. diffusa derived CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Boerhaavia diffusa plant was washed three times with Millipore water. It was chopped into slices and grinded with little amount of water. Cotton cloth was used to filter the extract and transfer it into a beaker. About 80 ml of the collected extract was transferred to a Teflon –lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 6 h and it was allowed to cool at room temperature. Once the extract had cooled, it was carefully removed from the Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and filtered through Whatmann No. 41 filter paper. The extract was cold centrifuged for an hour at 17,500 rpm to get rid of all the big particles. The big particles settled at the bottom of the centrifuge tube after centrifugation and were left with a dark brown coloured solution which were collected carefully from supernatant into a beaker. The beaker may contain some suspended colloidal particles and dialysis membrane was used in order to remove these suspended particles (A duplicate experiment was also conducted to calculate the yield of the carbon quantum dots. It is then freeze dried for 48 h and the yield of the carbon dots was determined from the original weight of the starting material (5 g of B. diffusa leaves) and the end weight of the carbon dots (0.489 g) was 9.78 %). For subsequent investigation, the aqueous solution of freshly made CQDs were then kept in a refrigerator at 4 °C.

The absorption spectra of CQDs were measured using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-1800 Series). A 200 kV high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) (JE-2100, JEOL) was used to study the size, shape and distribution of the particle sizes on the surface of the CQDs. The surface functional group of the generated CQDs was identified using an FTIR spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Infrared Spectrometer (IR Prestige 21). To analyze the crystallinity and graphitization of the CQDs, X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed using a powder diffractometer, Rigaku X-ray Machine (Miniflex 600), with monochromatized Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.54 Å) at 40 kV and 30 mA. The recorded data was in the range of 10–90° (2θ). 200 W monochromatic Al Kα radiation was used for X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) studies on a Thermo Scientific ESCA Lab 250Xi to identify elemental composition. The Horiba USA, Flurolog 3 TCSPC with a resolution of 0.3 nm fluorescence spectrophotometer was utilized to record the emission and excitation spectra of the CQDs via the use of a quartz cell with a 1 cm path length.

The metal selectivity of the generated CQDs was evaluated by measuring the photoluminescence intensity as a function of wavelength for different metal ions at ambient temperature. The synthesized carbon quantum dots were analysed for metal ion detection by adding different 1 µl nitrate solution of metal ions such as (Ca2+, Hg2+, K+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Sr2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Ni2+, Fe3+) using milli Q water. Subsequently, every CQD metal ion solution was placed in presence of UV to facilitate the fluorometric detection of metal ions. The pure carbon quantum dot solution exhibits fluorescence when exposed to UV light. The metal ions which were detected either showed fluorescence turn-off or fluorescence turn-on mechanism. It was determined that adding a 100 μM concentration of metal ion solution to the manufactured CQDs fully quenched their fluorescence.

The quantum yield (QY) of the produced CQDs were measured using a reference sample of quinine sulphate. 0.01 M H2SO4 was added to dissolve Quinine sulphate. The QY of the generated CQD was determined using the equation below [48];
(1)
QY % =
Q
Y R
I S
A R
n S
2
I R
A s
n R
2
where, R” and “S” represents the reference and sample, “I” represents measured integrated fluorescent emission intensity, “n” corresponds to refractive index of the solvent, and “A” refers to the absorbance at excited wavelength of 380 nm respectively. The estimated QY for the CQDs was approximately 21.2 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical and chemical characteristics of the CQDs derived from B. diffusa
ce:section.ce:para: There are numerous elemental sources of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen found in the Boerhaavia diffusa make it a great natural precursor material for the synthesis of carbon dots. Fig. 1 a displays the absorption spectrum of the CQDs, which was measured between 200 and 500 nm. It displayed a highest peak at 268 nm and a small peak at 330 nm corresponding to the n–π* transition of (CO) band and π–π* transition of (CC) band [49,50]. A prominent emission peak with a high intensity was clearly evident at 380 nm in the PL spectra of the CQDs displayed in Fig. 1b. Fig. 2 displays the analysed peaks of FT-IR spectra of which the strong and broad peak observed at 3466 cm−1 represents the vibration of the OH/NH band. The peak observed at 2847 cm−1 is due to the vibration mode of CH bond. The Presence of CO stretching is confirmed by the peak at 1636 cm−1 and the CN stretching is exhibited at peak 1355 cm−1. The vibrational spectra of the produced carbon quantum dots show a prominent band at 3400–3100 cm−1 that is caused by the stretching vibration of either the OH or NH groups. A weak band detected at 2847–2500 cm−1 indicates the existence of a nitrile group. [51,52].

The structure, phase, and degree of crystallinity of the synthesized CQDs were ascertained by XRD. The biosynthesized carbon dots from B. diffusa showed a diffraction peak at 25.7° in the XRD pattern. The broad single peak centered at 25.7° in Fig. 3 is comparable to those created using other methods, which show highly disordered carbon with a primarily amorphous structure and an interplanar spacing of 0.37 nm larger than the distance between 002 planes in bulk graphite (3.34 Å −0.33 nm). [51,53].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of temperature on the photoluminescence of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The temperature sensitive properties of the produced CQDs were conducted at room temperature. 1 ml of CQD solution was diluted with 2 ml of ultrapure water and incubated for 30 min each in a water bath oscillator set to a constant temperature (200 rpm/min) between 30 and 60 °C. The fluorescence intensity decreases as temperature increases. The CQDs were heated to 30–60 °C and then allowed to cool to room temperature in order to examine the change in fluorescence intensity and confirm the reversibility of CQD fluorescence intensity. The Fig. 4 indicates that the fluorescence intensity returned when the temperature was brought back down to 30 °C. This finding emphasizes the exceptional reversibility of CQDs by showing that their surface fluorescence structure is not permanently harmed by temperatures between 30 and 60 °C. It reveals that the produced CQD fluorescent nanoprobes show encouraging possibilities for creating a temperature sensor [54].

The distribution of size and shape of the CQD was determined by using HRTEM. Fig. 5 (a) and (b) indicate that the CQDs are spherical in shape and evenly distributed in solution, with an average size of approximately 5 nm. In transmission electron microscopy (TEM), the technique known as Selected Area Electron Diffraction (SAED) is used to investigate the crystallographic structure of materials, especially at the nanoscale. The SAED pattern gives information about the arrangement of atoms in a sample, which can be useful in identifying phases, crystal structures, and any defects in the material. The HRTEM image clearly shows that CQDs have a crystalline structure with a lattice spacing of 0.22 nm, indicating the presence of graphitic carbon's (SP2) 002-diffraction plane. Usually, the CDs have a diameter of less than 10 nm and the average particle size of B. diffusa derived CQDs in this study is 5 nm [51,54]. The analysis demonstrates a strong correlation, with a deviation of only 5–7 % among particle size measurements obtained by TEM and XRD for highly monodisperse, quasi spherical and crystalline nano particles. Every technique has benefits of its own, and they can all be used effectively together [55].

The presence of various elements and nature of their bonds were examined using XPS in Fig. 6 (a) shows peaks for the presence of Oxygen (293 eV), Nitrogen (291 eV), and Carbon (284 eV). In Fig. 6(b), the fitted C1s peaks CC observed at 284 eV, OCO Observed at 291 eV, π–π* corresponds to 293 eV may be ascribed to the presence of sp2 carbon. In Fig. 6(c), the fitted N1s peak with their corresponding binding energy of 393 eV can be related to CNC indicates that the Nitrogen. Whereas in Fig. 6(d), the fitted O1s peaks with their corresponding binding energies of 530 eV can be related to CO and 532.3 eV can be ascribed to COH, COC respectively [56].

The selectivity of B. diffusa CQDs were tested against different metal ion solutions such as (Ca2+, Hg2+, K+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Sr2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Ni2+, Fe3+) in Fig. 7 (a). It is identified that the metal ion Fe3+ has a significant impact on the luminescence intensity of CQD whereas the other metal ions have very little effect. The interference study of Fe3+ions with other metal ions were also conducted in Fig. 7(b). In this experiment, 1 ml of the CQD solution diluted with 2 ml of water in which 1–10 µl of neutral ferric chloride was added and fluorescence spectrum was measured at 380 nm Fig. 7(c). This graph indciates that the lumiscence intensity of the CQDs decreases with an increase in Fe3+ ion concentration which indicates the luminescence intensity is dependent on concentration of Fe3+ ions.

The fluorescence quenching efficiency was investigated using the Stern–Volmer, F0/F = 1 + KSV [Q] plot (a strong linear curve, R2 = 0.9969) in the concentration range of 1–10 μl to investigate the sensitivity of CQD on concentrations of Fe3+ in Fig. 7(d) [57,58].

The Limit of Detection (LOD) was determined by recording the variation in luminescence intensity of B. diffusa CQDs as a function of concentration of Fe3+ ions which is calculated using the following equation
LOD =
3 × S t a n d a r d d e v i a t i o n
Slope
The Limit of detection of the produced CQDs was calculated to be 0.741 µM with a standard deviation of R2 = 0.9865. The LOD of other photoluminescent materials is described below. Table 1 .

The CQD sensing mechanism consists of the non-radiative relaxation process and photoluminescence quenching. The excited electrons return to the ground state in the absence of metal ions by radiating out their excess energy. The fluorescence emission intensity was greatly suppressed when Fe3+ ions were added to the prepared CQDs. This strong quenching behaviour could be the result of the formation of CQDs-Fe3+ complexes. Coordination complexes are formed between the interaction with metal ions and functional groups (e.g., OH, COOH, and NH 2 groups) on the surface of the CQDs [61–63]. Moreover, numerous researchers suggested that Fe3+ can chelate CQDs rapidly and has a strong thermodynamic affinity [64,65]. The PL quenching characteristic would probably arise from the coordinated Fe-CQDs complex developing to improve charge transfer and inhibit exciton recombination.

This paper presented a straightforward, organic, and time-efficient approach for the hydrothermal synthesis of carbon quantum dots from Beorhaavia Diffusa for the first time and its structural, morphological and optical characteristics were analysed by various spectroscopic techniques. The obtained C-dots are highly fluorescent and having an average size of 5 nm. It was observed that the highly luminescent CQDs was then successfully utilized to monitor the quenching effect of fluorescence in order to selectively detect Fe3+ ions with a low LOD of 0.741 µM. With a high QY of 21.2 %, the produced CQDs showed quenching effect at high concentrations and excitation-dependent emission behaviour. Based on these significant findings, it can be concluded that CQD produced from Boerhaavia Diffusa represent a novel kind of fluorescence probe with bright futures in the field of environmental monitoring and sensor development. Utilizing the unique properties of this medicinal plant, carbon dots have been developed by a low cost environmentally benign synthesis procedure that exhibits remarkable ferric ion detection capabilities.
Jincy Jose: Writing – original draft, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Rangaswamy Mohanraj: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources.
This is an observational study. The Bharathidasn University Research Ethics Committee has confirmed that no ethical approval is required.
The authors did not receive financial support from any organization for the submitted work.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Ms. Jincy Jose, is a M Phil graduate in Environmental Science and currently doing her PhD in Environmental Science under the guidance of Dr. R. Mohanraj in the Department of Environmental Science & Management, Bharathidasan University Tiruchirapally, Tamilnadu, India. Her research focuses on the design and synthesis of green carbon quantum dots for metal ion sensing applications. She has received the Commonwealth Professional Fellowship, funded by Government of UK in 2016 in the area of environment. She can be contacted at jkattupalam@gcom.

Dr. Rangaswamy Mohanraj is a Professor of Environmental Sciences at Bharathidasan University, Tiruchirappalli, India. His primary research interest lies on Environmental Impact and Risk Assessment, Urban Air Pollution, Hydro-Geochemistry, Ecological Restoration, Forest Carbon Dynamics, and Corporate Environmental Management. As a principal investigator he had completed five major externally funded research projects [projects funded by DST, UGC and DRDO (Govt. of India)]; and presently carrying out two major externally funded projects including DST-SERB (CRG) and an All-India coordinated project funded by ICFRE (MoEF&CC, GoI). He had also completed 25 Environmental Impact Assessment/Consultancy projects as a Principal Investigator and Co-Investigator. He had successfully guided 7 PhD scholars in Environmental Science and Environmental Management. He can be contacted at mohan@bdu.ac.in.
